sub: Technology block (c): asynchronous communication between the systems that must never wait for each other

title: Why Message Queues?
desc: A system for asynchronous communication between applications

items: Asynchronous communication: the sender does not wait for the receiver
In the Java world specified by JMS (Java Message Service), part of Java/Jakarta EE
Decoupling — sender and receiver work independently; a UTM service can restart without losing telemetry
Load distribution — messages are processed in parallel by many consumers
Reliability — persistent storage of messages until they are consumed
Scalability — add consumers dynamically as traffic grows

title: JMS Architecture: Queues & Topics
desc: Two delivery models cover almost every messaging need

body.code:
Point-to-Point (Queue)              Publish-Subscribe (Topic)

Producer                             Publisher
   |                                     |
   v                                     v
[ QUEUE ] --> exactly one            [ TOPIC ] --> every subscriber
              consumer gets              |    \
              each message           Subscriber A  Subscriber B

clearance requests,                  position broadcasts,
work distribution                    airspace status updates

items: Message producer — sends messages to a queue or topic; message consumer — receives them
Message queue — waiting line for messages (point-to-point model)

rows: The classic choice for JMS in Java projects
Supports JMS via plugins; very flexible routing
Distributed log (not JMS, but similar use cases)
Massively scalable streaming — the natural fit for high-volume telemetry

items: JMS makes asynchronous communication in Java EE applications straightforward
Different implementations offer different guarantees — pick by throughput, ordering and persistence needs
Key concepts to remember: producer, consumer, queue, topic
In our domain: telemetry fan-out is a topic, clearance processing is a queue
